questions.q: In Hindu tradition, who is the divine consort of the goddess Parvati?
questions.explanation: Parvati is revered in the Puranas as the consort of Shiva, the great ascetic of Mount Kailash.

questions.q: The name 'Parvati' and her epithet 'Daughter of the Mountain' refer to her birth as the daughter of which mountain king?
questions.explanation: Parvati is the daughter of Himavan (Himalaya), lord of the Himalayas, which is why she is called Parvati, 'she of the mountain.'

questions.q: According to the Puranas, the goddess Parvati is regarded as the reincarnation of which earlier consort of Shiva?
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that Parvati is the reborn soul of Sati, Shiva's first wife, who had given up her body at Daksha's sacrifice.

questions.q: Which elephant-headed deity is celebrated in the Puranas as a son of Parvati?
questions.explanation: Ganesha, the elephant-headed remover of obstacles, is honoured in the Puranas as the son of Parvati and Shiva.

questions.q: The war-god Kartikeya, who leads the armies of the gods, is described in the Puranas as a son of which goddess?
questions.explanation: Kartikeya (also called Skanda or Murugan), commander of the divine armies, is revered as the son of Parvati and Shiva.

questions.q: By what gentle, golden-hued name is the goddess Parvati commonly known in her benevolent form?
questions.explanation: In her gentle, fair-complexioned aspect Parvati is widely known as Gauri, 'the radiant one.'

questions.q: The Puranas describe Parvati winning Shiva as her husband through years of what spiritual discipline?
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that Parvati performed years of severe tapas (ascetic austerity) to win Shiva as her husband.

questions.q: Parvati is honoured as a form of which encompassing divine principle in Hindu tradition?
questions.explanation: Parvati is venerated as a form of the Great Goddess, the Devi or Shakti who embodies the divine feminine energy.

questions.q: Which fierce warrior goddess, slayer of the buffalo-demon, is regarded in the Puranas as a form of Parvati?
questions.explanation: Durga, the lion-riding slayer of the buffalo-demon Mahishasura, is counted among the fierce forms of Parvati.

questions.q: Parvati and Shiva are said in the Puranas to dwell together upon which sacred Himalayan mountain?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe Parvati dwelling with Shiva upon Mount Kailash, his Himalayan abode.

questions.q: By which ancient one-syllable name, traditionally said to mean 'O, do not (practise such austerity),' is the goddess Parvati also widely known?
questions.explanation: Parvati is widely revered under the name Uma, an epithet for the Daughter of the Mountain used across the Puranas and Kalidasa's poetry.

questions.q: In the dark, fearsome form associated with Parvati, the goddess Kali is most often depicted with what complexion?
questions.explanation: Kali, a fierce form linked to Parvati, is depicted with a dark or black complexion, embodying the terrifying aspect of the Goddess.

questions.q: According to a well-known Puranic episode, how did Parvati first bring forth her son Ganesha to guard her doorway?
options: She fashioned him from turmeric paste of her own body
She forged him from divine fire
She received him as a boon from Brahma
He sprang fully grown from the Ganga
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that Parvati created Ganesha from the paste (often turmeric or unguent) of her own body and gave him life to guard her door.

questions.q: In the Puranic tale of Ganesha's birth, what did Shiva do to the boy when he barred Shiva's entry, before restoring him?
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that Shiva beheaded the boy guarding Parvati's door, then restored him to life with an elephant's head.

questions.q: Parvati's union with Shiva is said to harmonise which two ideals of Hindu life?
questions.explanation: As the consort who draws Shiva into family life, Parvati unites the ascetic (yogi) and householder (grihastha) ideals.

questions.q: In her form as the warrior goddess Durga, which animal does the goddess linked to Parvati ride as her mount?
questions.explanation: As Durga, the goddess associated with Parvati rides a lion (or tiger) into battle against the demons.

questions.q: Which god of love, in the Puranic account, was burned to ash by Shiva when he tried to kindle Shiva's desire for Parvati?
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that Shiva burned Kamadeva, the god of love, to ashes with his third eye for disturbing his meditation as Parvati sought him.

questions.q: The half-male, half-female icon Ardhanarishvara unites Shiva with which goddess as a single divine body?
questions.explanation: Ardhanarishvara depicts Shiva and Parvati fused as one androgynous form, symbolising the inseparability of the divine masculine and feminine.

questions.q: In the Puranas, who was the father of Sati, whose death and self-sacrifice precede Parvati's birth?
questions.explanation: Sati, whom Parvati is reborn as, was the daughter of Daksha, at whose sacrifice she gave up her body.

questions.q: Many of the great episodes of Parvati's life, including her tapas and marriage to Shiva, are chiefly preserved in which body of Hindu scripture?
questions.explanation: The narratives of Parvati's austerities, marriage, and motherhood are chiefly elaborated in the Puranas.

questions.q: Besides Ganesha, which other son is named in the Puranas as a child of Parvati and Shiva?
questions.explanation: Kartikeya, the six-faced war-god also called Skanda, is the other son of Parvati and Shiva named in the Puranas.

questions.q: Because Parvati is the active power through whom Shiva acts, she is identified with which Sanskrit principle?
questions.explanation: Parvati is identified with Shakti, the dynamic divine energy without which Shiva is held to be inert.

questions.q: In the classical Sanskrit poem 'Kumarasambhava' by Kalidasa, whose birth from Parvati and Shiva is the central subject?
questions.explanation: Kalidasa's 'Kumarasambhava' ('The Birth of Kumara') narrates the courtship of Shiva and Parvati and the birth of Kumara, that is Kartikeya.

questions.q: The Puranas tell that Parvati once playfully covered Shiva's eyes, plunging the cosmos into darkness, and was therefore born dark as which goddess until purified?
questions.explanation: A Puranic tale holds that for covering Shiva's eyes Parvati took on a dark form as Kali, later shedding it to become the fair Gauri.

questions.q: In the Devi Mahatmya, the fierce goddess Chamunda, linked to Parvati, slays which pair of demon generals from whom her name derives?
questions.explanation: The Devi Mahatmya relates that the goddess gained the name Chamunda by slaying the demons Chanda and Munda.

questions.q: In Kalidasa's account, while Parvati performed her austerities, Shiva tested her by appearing disguised as what figure who spoke ill of himself?
questions.explanation: In the 'Kumarasambhava', Shiva tests Parvati's devotion by coming disguised as a young brahmachari who disparages Shiva, only for her to defend him.

questions.q: The fierce goddess Durga, a form of Parvati, is most celebrated in the Devi Mahatmya for slaying which buffalo-shaped demon?
questions.explanation: The Devi Mahatmya extols Durga, a form of Parvati, as the slayer of the buffalo-demon Mahishasura.

questions.q: Parvati is sometimes called Annapurna, in which role she is worshipped as the goddess of what?
questions.explanation: As Annapurna, Parvati is venerated as the goddess of nourishment and food, who feeds and sustains all beings.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, Parvati and Shiva's son Kartikeya was born expressly to slay which demon who oppressed the gods?
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that Kartikeya was born to Parvati and Shiva to destroy the demon Tarakasura, whom only their son could kill.

questions.q: Parvati's marriage to Shiva is, in the Puranas, understood to also bring about the conditions for the birth of the divine general needed to defeat the demons, a union urged by which assembly?
options: The merchant guilds of the earth
The gods (devas) led by Indra and Brahma
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that the gods, led by Indra and Brahma, sought the marriage of Shiva and Parvati so their son could vanquish the demon Taraka.
